# Investigative Narrative — Case SRL18-RD-NET

*Generated by Council-SIFT from its verification substrate (`claw-memory-core`). Every claim below is
**COUNCIL_VERIFIED**: it survived adversarial review by the forensic seats, the evidence it cites resolves
in real tool output, and it is traceable to the exact tool execution; concrete-command receipts may be rerun with `csift trace --rerun`, while stored-output rehash remains the baseline integrity check.*

5 of 8 drafted finding(s) are Council-verified; 3 draft(s) were

verified picture:

## Findings

### F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-001 — windows.netscan shows local sockets on this host bound to two d… *(confidence: MEDIUM)*

windows.netscan shows local sockets on this host bound to two distinct private IPv4 addresses: 10.10.6.15 (e.g. ncpa_listener. LISTENING on 10.10.6.15:5693; java.exe ESTABLISHED 10.10.6.15:49203 -> 10.10.200.207:5672) and 172.16.6.15 (e.g. System NetBIOS UDP on 172.16.6.15:137 and :138; and 172.16.6.15:3262 ESTABLISHED to 172.16.5.50). svchost.exe PID 392 is LISTENING on 0.0.0.0:3389 (TCPv4 and TCPv6) and System (PID 4) is LISTENING on 0.0.0.0:445.. The host is dual-homed, with interfaces in both the 10.10.6.0 and 172.16.6.0 segments, and exposes the RDP service (TCP 3389) and SMB (TCP 445). A machine straddling two subnets that also serves RDP/SMB is well positioned to bridge the segments and act as a lateral-movement pivot. No ESTABLISHED inbound session on 3389 appears in this scan, so an active interactive RDP session at the moment of capture is NOT evidenced by netscan alone..

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/rd05/base-rd-05-memory.img#netscan offsets 0x33d4310, 0xbc1024d0, 0xbbf88a30, 0x3392800, 0xbc0ae9e0; IPs 10.10.6.15 and 172.16.6.15` — tool `volatility3 windows.netscan`, command `vol -f /tmp/rd05/base-rd-05-memory.img windows.netscan`
- **Cited evidence:** `10.10.6.15`, `172.16.6.15`, `5693`, `10.10.200.207`, `172.16.5.50`, `3389`, `445`, `LISTENING`, `svchost.exe` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 974fc6d7847b1a77…` — stored-output re-hash verified by `csift trace F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-001`; `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-001` is the stronger independent check when the recorded command is concrete and the evidence path is present.
- **Council Receipt:** `920601971fc74e42…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

### F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-003 — windows.netscan shows PID 22456 'subject_srv.ex' LISTENING on 0… *(confidence: MEDIUM)*

windows.netscan shows PID 22456 'subject_srv.ex' LISTENING on 0.0.0.0:3262 (TCPv4 offset 0xbbe3f620, plus the matching TCPv6 ::3262), and an ESTABLISHED TCPv4 session 172.16.6.15:3262 with foreign address 172.16.5.50:43036 (offset 0xbbf88a30) owned by the same PID 22456.. A process named subject_srv.exe is bound to an uncommon TCP port (3262) and has a live inbound connection from another internal host, 172.16.5.50. The remote side's high ephemeral source port (43036) terminating on local port 3262 is consistent with 172.16.5.50 acting as the CLIENT of a custom service running on this host -- an internal peer that reached this listener over the network. A custom listening service of this kind can serve as a remote-access channel, and it is the named subject of this case, so it warrants follow-up. This netscan output alone evidences only the live socket and its single internal peer; it does not by itself characterise the traffic carried over the session as malicious.. This is the **corrected** finding — an initial, stronger reading was refuted by the Council (see *Self-correction*, below) and the analyst re-filed only what the evidence supports.

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/rd05/base-rd-05-memory.img#netscan offsets 0xbbe3f620 (LISTENING 3262) and 0xbbf88a30 (ESTABLISHED 172.16.6.15:3262<-172.16.5.50:43036); PID 22456` — tool `volatility3 windows.netscan`, command `vol -f /tmp/rd05/base-rd-05-memory.img windows.netscan`
- **Cited evidence:** `subject_srv.ex`, `3262`, `172.16.6.15`, `172.16.5.50`, `43036`, `ESTABLISHED`, `LISTENING`, `22456` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 974fc6d7847b1a77…` — stored-output re-hash verified by `csift trace F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-003`; `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-003` is the stronger independent check when the recorded command is concrete and the evidence path is present.
- **Council Receipt:** `6bce5dd2eb5b6691…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

### F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-004 — windows.netscan lists a TCPv4 endpoint (offset 0xbda06cf0) with… *(confidence: MEDIUM)*

windows.netscan lists a TCPv4 endpoint (offset 0xbda06cf0) with local port 59638 and foreign address 10.10.5.28 foreign port 5985, state CLOSED, owned by PID 392 svchost.exe. The same scan shows this host LISTENING locally on 0.0.0.0:5985 (System, PID 4) and 0.0.0.0:47001 (System).. TCP 5985 is the WinRM / Windows Remote Management (PowerShell Remoting) HTTP port. In this entry the FOREIGN port is 5985 while the LOCAL port is an ephemeral 59638, which indicates this host acted as the WinRM CLIENT toward 10.10.5.28 -- an OUTBOUND remote-management connection FROM this host TO 10.10.5.28. The CLOSED state means the session was torn down before capture. This is consistent with this host being used to remotely manage/execute against 10.10.5.28 (outbound lateral movement). CAVEAT: netscan evidences the socket and peer, not the commands carried over the WinRM session.. This is the **corrected** finding — an initial, stronger reading was refuted by the Council (see *Self-correction*, below) and the analyst re-filed only what the evidence supports.

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/rd05/base-rd-05-memory.img#netscan offset 0xbda06cf0 (local 59638 -> 10.10.5.28:5985 CLOSED, PID 392)` — tool `volatility3 windows.netscan`, command `vol -f /tmp/rd05/base-rd-05-memory.img windows.netscan`
- **Cited evidence:** `59638`, `10.10.5.28`, `5985`, `CLOSED`, `svchost.exe`, `392`, `47001` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 974fc6d7847b1a77…` — stored-output re-hash verified by `csift trace F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-004`; `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-004` is the stronger independent check when the recorded command is concrete and the evidence path is present.
- **Council Receipt:** `99991e753d49a0f7…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

### F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-007 — windows.psscan lists PID 17612 powershell.exe with parent PID 3… *(confidence: MEDIUM)*

windows.psscan lists PID 17612 powershell.exe with parent PID 3416 (CreateTime 2018-08-31 00:55:20 UTC), and lists PID 3416 as WmiPrvSE.exe with parent PID 796. The same scan lists a further powershell.exe PID 17144 whose parent PID is 17612.. WmiPrvSE.exe (PID 3416) is the WMI Provider Host. In this scan powershell.exe (PID 17612) is recorded as its child, and a second powershell.exe (PID 17144) as a child of 17612. The presence of PowerShell beneath the WMI Provider Host is an artifact worth examining when assessing whether WMI was used to create processes on this host, and is recorded here as an investigative lead. This output reflects process parent/child lineage only; it does not show the command line that ran, where any request originated, or whether the activity was benign or malicious, so it is reported for corroboration rather than as a conclusion.. This is the **corrected** finding — an initial, stronger reading was refuted by the Council (see *Self-correction*, below) and the analyst re-filed only what the evidence supports.

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/rd05/base-rd-05-memory.img#psscan PIDs 17612 (powershell, offset 0xbd420500), 3416 (WmiPrvSE, offset 0xbb4f2690), 17144 (powershell, offset 0xbc95b060)` — tool `volatility3 windows.psscan`, command `vol -f /tmp/rd05/base-rd-05-memory.img windows.psscan`
- **Cited evidence:** `powershell.exe`, `17612`, `3416`, `WmiPrvSE.exe`, `796`, `17144` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 6b8dac2f0149ec84…` — stored-output re-hash verified by `csift trace F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-007`; `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-007` is the stronger independent check when the recorded command is concrete and the evidence path is present.
- **Council Receipt:** `caf4428ba9f445a3…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

### F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-008 — windows.netscan records an ESTABLISHED TCPv4 session (offset 0x… *(confidence: LOW)*

windows.netscan records an ESTABLISHED TCPv4 session (offset 0xbdee9cf0) from 10.10.6.15:59486 to foreign address 10.10.254.1 foreign port 61613, owned by PID 19688 rubyw.exe. The same scan also records an ESTABLISHED TCPv4 session (offset 0xbc1024d0) from 10.10.6.15:49203 to 10.10.200.207:5672 owned by PID 1736 java.exe.. rubyw.exe (PID 19688) is the windowless Ruby interpreter; netscan shows it holding a live outbound TCP session from this host to internal host 10.10.254.1 on port 61613. A general-purpose scripting interpreter holding a network session is an artifact worth reviewing, because such interpreters can host either legitimate automation or attacker tooling. Port 61613 is commonly associated with STOMP messaging and the co-resident java.exe session to 10.10.200.207:5672 is commonly AMQP, so the two may belong to a message-bus client stack. This netscan output reflects only the socket endpoints and their live state; it does not reveal the data carried or whether the activity is benign, and is recorded as a lead for corroboration rather than a conclusion.. This is the **corrected** finding — an initial, stronger reading was refuted by the Council (see *Self-correction*, below) and the analyst re-filed only what the evidence supports.

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/rd05/base-rd-05-memory.img#netscan offset 0xbdee9cf0 (10.10.6.15:59486 -> 10.10.254.1:61613, PID 19688 rubyw.exe); cf. 0xbc1024d0 (java.exe PID 1736 -> 10.10.200.207:5672)` — tool `volatility3 windows.netscan`, command `vol -f /tmp/rd05/base-rd-05-memory.img windows.netscan`
- **Cited evidence:** `rubyw.exe`, `19688`, `10.10.6.15`, `59486`, `10.10.254.1`, `61613`, `ESTABLISHED`, `java.exe`, `10.10.200.207`, `5672` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 974fc6d7847b1a77…` — stored-output re-hash verified by `csift trace F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-008`; `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-008` is the stronger independent check when the recorded command is concrete and the evidence path is present.
- **Council Receipt:** `9f0570de4239d46f…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

## What the evidence does NOT support (Council-enforced discipline)

The Council refused the following stronger readings because the available artifacts do not establish them.
They are recorded here explicitly so the report distinguishes **confirmed** from **inferred or unfounded**:

- Inference overreach (CERTAINTY): unjustified certainty — a single artifact rarely proves a conclusion outright. Offending clause: "CAVEAT: netscan proves only the live socket and its single internal peer".
- Inference overreach (ATTRIBUTION): attribution to a specific actor cannot be established from one artifact — corroborate with intel + multiple artifacts. Offending clause: "PowerShell launched under WmiPrvSE is the classic process-lineage signature of remote command execution via WMI (e".
- Cited token(s) NOT present in the tool output as a standalone token (hallucinated/unsupported): powershell.exe, 17612, 3416, WmiPrvSE.exe, 796, 17144.
- Inference overreach (CAUSATION): causation asserted from correlation — the artifact shows state, not cause. Offending clause: "Such WMI-driven execution can be triggered either locally or remotely".

## Self-correction record (no human intervention)

- **F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-002** was drafted as: *"windows.netscan shows PID 22456 'subject_srv.ex' LISTENING on 0.0.0.0:3262 (TCP… → A process named subject_srv.exe is bound to an uncommon TCP port (3262) and has a live in…"*, then **bounced** by the Council:
  > [seat:inference] Inference overreach (CERTAINTY): unjustified certainty — a single artifact rarely proves a conclusion outright. Offending clause: "CAVEAT: netscan proves only the live socket and its single internal peer".

  The analyst read the objection and self-corrected — re-filing the supported claim now shown under *Findings*.

  > [seat:inference] Inference overreach (ATTRIBUTION): attribution to a specific actor cannot be established from one artifact — corroborate with intel + multiple artifacts. Offending clause: "PowerShell launched under WmiPrvSE is the classic…

  The analyst read the objection and self-corrected — re-filing the supported claim now shown under *Findings*.
- **F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-006** was drafted as: *"windows.psscan lists PID 17612 powershell.exe with parent PID 3416 (CreateTime … → powershell.exe (PID 17612) is a direct child of WmiPrvSE.exe (the WMI Provider Host, PID …"*, then **bounced** by the Council:
  > [seat:citation] Cited token(s) NOT present in the tool output as a standalone token (hallucinated/unsupported): powershell.exe, 17612, 3416, WmiPrvSE.exe, 796, 17144. [seat:inference] Inference overreach (CAUSATION): causation asserted fro…

  The analyst read the objection and self-corrected — re-filing the supported claim now shown under *Findings*.

## Confidence & limitations

- Confidence is stated per finding (HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW / SPECULATIVE) and reflects what a single artifact
  can bear; nothing here is asserted beyond the cited evidence.
- "Observation" is the literal tool output; "interpretation" is the analyst's inference. The Council's
  Citation, Tool-semantics, Contradiction, Inference, and Scope seats independently checked the leap between them; Synthesis aggregated the verdicts.
- Every finding traces to its tool execution (`csift trace F-analyst-SRL18-RD-NET-001`); concrete-command receipts can additionally be re-executed against evidence with `csift trace --rerun …`. Council Receipts are hash-chained.
- This narrative is generated from the audit substrate, not hand-written — it cannot contain a claim that
  is not a verified node in the graph.
